1.lecture:
§1 · A vector is a magnitude with a direction

A vector carries two pieces of information at once — how much and which way — and in three dimensions it is written by its components \( \mathbf{a}=(a_1,a_2,a_3) \). Its magnitude is the ordinary distance formula \( |\mathbf{a}|=\sqrt{a_1^2+a_2^2+a_3^2} \); for \( \mathbf{a}=(3,4,0) \) this is \( \sqrt{9+16}=5 \). Dividing a vector by its own length produces a unit vector \( \hat{\mathbf{a}}=\mathbf{a}/|\mathbf{a}| \) that keeps the direction but has length one, so \( \hat{\mathbf{a}}=(0.6,\,0.8,\,0) \) here. Unit vectors are the currency of direction in engineering: a pipe axis, a surface normal, a line of action are all specified by a unit vector, and every projection and angle calculation below is really a statement about them. The whole of 3-D statics — the force balances of the last course — rests on being fluent with components, magnitudes, and unit vectors.

§2 · The dot product measures alignment — and gives work

The dot product multiplies two vectors and returns a single number: \( \mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{b}=a_1b_1+a_2b_2+a_3b_3 \), which equals \( |\mathbf{a}||\mathbf{b}|\cos\theta \). It measures how much the two vectors point the same way. When they are perpendicular the cosine is zero, so a zero dot product is the algebraic test for a right angle — the single most used fact in the entire subject. Its physical reading is work: a force \( \mathbf{F} \) moving through a displacement \( \mathbf{d} \) does work \( W=\mathbf{F}\cdot\mathbf{d} \), counting only the part of the force along the motion. A force \( \mathbf{F}=(120,90,0) \) N pushing a block through \( \mathbf{d}=(2,0,0) \) m does \( W=120(2)+90(0)=240 \) J; the \(90\)-N cross-component, perpendicular to the motion, contributes nothing.

Key identity — dot product

\[ \mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{b}=a_1b_1+a_2b_2+a_3b_3=|\mathbf{a}||\mathbf{b}|\cos\theta \]

Reads as alignment. \( \mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{b}=0 \iff \) the vectors are perpendicular. Physical use: work \( W=\mathbf{F}\cdot\mathbf{d} \).

§3 · Angles and projections

Because \( \mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{b}=|\mathbf{a}||\mathbf{b}|\cos\theta \), the angle between two vectors is recovered directly: \( \cos\theta=\dfrac{\mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{b}}{|\mathbf{a}||\mathbf{b}|} \). For \( \mathbf{a}=(3,4,0) \) and the \(x\)-axis \( (1,0,0) \), \( \cos\theta=3/5=0.6 \), so \( \theta\approx53.13^\circ \). The related idea is the projection of \( \mathbf{a} \) onto \( \mathbf{b} \), the shadow \( \mathbf{a} \) casts along \( \mathbf{b} \): its length is \( \dfrac{\mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{b}}{|\mathbf{b}|} \) and the vector itself is \( \operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf{b}}\mathbf{a}=\dfrac{\mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{b}}{\mathbf{b}\cdot\mathbf{b}}\,\mathbf{b} \). Projection is how a force is resolved along a chosen axis, how a velocity is split into along-track and cross-track parts, and how the "component in a direction" that statics kept asking for is computed honestly rather than by inspection.

§4 · The cross product measures area — and gives moment

The cross product of two 3-D vectors returns a third vector, perpendicular to both, with magnitude \( |\mathbf{a}\times\mathbf{b}|=|\mathbf{a}||\mathbf{b}|\sin\theta \) equal to the area of the parallelogram they span, and direction set by the right-hand rule. Its supreme engineering use is the moment (torque) of a force about a point: \( \mathbf{M}=\mathbf{r}\times\mathbf{F} \), where \( \mathbf{r} \) is the position from the pivot to the force's line of action. A \(200\)-N force at \( \mathbf{r}=(0.5,0,0) \) m, acting in the \(+y\) direction \( (0,200,0) \), produces \( \mathbf{M}=\mathbf{r}\times\mathbf{F}=(0,0,100) \) N·m — a \(100\) N·m twist about the \(z\)-axis, turning counter-clockwise. Where the dot product discarded the perpendicular part, the cross product keeps only it: the two products are complementary halves of one geometry.

Key identity — cross product & moment

\[ |\mathbf{a}\times\mathbf{b}|=|\mathbf{a}||\mathbf{b}|\sin\theta=\text{area spanned};\qquad \mathbf{M}=\mathbf{r}\times\mathbf{F} \]

Direction ⟂ to both, by the right-hand rule. \( \mathbf{a}\times\mathbf{b}=\mathbf{0} \iff \) the vectors are parallel.

§5 · Lines and planes in space

Vectors give the cleanest description of geometry. A line through a point \( \mathbf{r}_0 \) along a direction \( \mathbf{v} \) is the set \( \mathbf{r}=\mathbf{r}_0+t\,\mathbf{v} \) as the parameter \( t \) runs over all reals. A plane is fixed by a point \( \mathbf{r}_0 \) and a normal vector \( \mathbf{n} \) perpendicular to it: every in-plane displacement \( \mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}_0 \) satisfies \( \mathbf{n}\cdot(\mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}_0)=0 \), which expands to \( n_1x+n_2y+n_3z=d \). With \( \mathbf{n}=(2,-1,2) \) through \( (1,1,1) \), \( d=\mathbf{n}\cdot\mathbf{r}_0=3 \), giving \( 2x-y+2z=3 \). The perpendicular distance from any point \( \mathbf{q} \) to that plane is \( \dfrac{|\mathbf{n}\cdot\mathbf{q}-d|}{|\mathbf{n}|} \); the origin sits \( |{-}3|/3=1 \) unit away. Clearances, interference checks, and contact analysis in CAD are all this one formula.

Key results — lines & planes

Line: \( \mathbf{r}=\mathbf{r}_0+t\mathbf{v} \). Plane: \( \mathbf{n}\cdot(\mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}_0)=0 \Rightarrow n_1x+n_2y+n_3z=d \).

Point-to-plane distance: \( \dfrac{|\mathbf{n}\cdot\mathbf{q}-d|}{|\mathbf{n}|} \).

§6 · The two products, side by side

Keep the division clean and every 3-D problem sorts itself. The dot product is a number that peaks when vectors align and vanishes at right angles — reach for it for work, for components, for testing perpendicularity, for angles. The cross product is a vector that vanishes when vectors align and peaks at right angles — reach for it for moment, for area, for a normal to two directions, for testing parallelism. Almost every quantity in statics, dynamics, and electromagnetism is one of these two contractions of a pair of vectors, and choosing the right one is usually the whole battle. The scalar triple product \( \mathbf{a}\cdot(\mathbf{b}\times\mathbf{c}) \) combines both to give the signed volume of the parallelepiped on three vectors — the determinant test for whether three directions are independent, which the linear-algebra lessons ahead build on directly.

§7 · Computing the cross product in practice

By hand, the cross product is read off a symbolic determinant with the unit vectors in the top row:

\[ \mathbf{a}\times\mathbf{b}=\det\begin{pmatrix}\hat{\imath}&\hat{\jmath}&\hat{k}\\a_1&a_2&a_3\\b_1&b_2&b_3\end{pmatrix} \]

which expands, component by component, to \( (a_2b_3-a_3b_2,\;a_3b_1-a_1b_3,\;a_1b_2-a_2b_1) \). This is the formula behind every moment in the lesson, and it is worth memorising as a pattern — each component skips its own index and cross-multiplies the other two. The determinant view also explains why \( \mathbf{a}\times\mathbf{b}=-\,\mathbf{b}\times\mathbf{a} \): swapping two rows of a determinant flips its sign, so the cross product is anti-commutative, and the order of \( \mathbf{r} \) and \( \mathbf{F} \) in a moment therefore fixes the sense of the twist. Getting that order right is the difference between a bolt that tightens and one that loosens.

quiz.q:
Work and angle from the dot product

Problem. A force \( \mathbf{F}=(120,90,0) \) N drags a skid through a displacement \( \mathbf{d}=(2,0,0) \) m. Find (a) the work done, and (b) the angle between \( \mathbf{F} \) and the motion.
quiz.solution: Solution. (a) \( W=\mathbf{F}\cdot\mathbf{d}=120(2)+90(0)+0=240 \) J. (b) \( |\mathbf{F}|=\sqrt{120^2+90^2}=\sqrt{22500}=150 \) N and \( |\mathbf{d}|=2 \) m, so \( \cos\theta=\dfrac{W}{|\mathbf{F}||\mathbf{d}|}=\dfrac{240}{300}=0.8 \), giving \( \theta=36.87^\circ \). Only the \(120\)-N along-track component does work; the \(90\)-N cross-component is carried for free.

quiz.q:
Moment by the cross product

Problem. A \(200\) N force acts in the \(+y\) direction at position \( \mathbf{r}=(0.5,0,0) \) m from a bolt. Find the moment about the bolt.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( \mathbf{M}=\mathbf{r}\times\mathbf{F}=(0.5,0,0)\times(0,200,0) \). Using \( \mathbf{M}=(r_2F_3-r_3F_2,\;r_3F_1-r_1F_3,\;r_1F_2-r_2F_1) \): the first two components are \(0\), and \( M_z=0.5(200)-0=100 \). So \( \mathbf{M}=(0,0,100) \) N·m — a \(100\) N·m torque about \(z\), counter-clockwise by the right-hand rule.

quiz.q:
Plane and clearance

Problem. A machined face passes through \( (1,1,1) \) with normal \( \mathbf{n}=(2,-1,2) \). Write the plane's equation and find the perpendicular distance from the origin.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( d=\mathbf{n}\cdot\mathbf{r}_0=2(1)-1(1)+2(1)=3 \), so the plane is \( 2x-y+2z=3 \). With \( |\mathbf{n}|=\sqrt{2^2+(-1)^2+2^2}=\sqrt{9}=3 \), the distance from the origin is \( \dfrac{|\mathbf{n}\cdot\mathbf{0}-d|}{|\mathbf{n}|}=\dfrac{|-3|}{3}=1 \) unit.

quiz.q: The magnitude of \( (3,4,0) \) is:
Two nonzero vectors are perpendicular exactly when:
quiz.solution: \( \mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{b}=|\mathbf{a}||\mathbf{b}|\cos\theta \); it is zero iff \( \cos\theta=0 \), i.e. \( \theta=90^\circ \).

quiz.q: The work done by \( \mathbf{F}=(120,90,0) \) N over \( \mathbf{d}=(2,0,0) \) m is:

quiz.solution: The cross product is \( (0,0,6) \); its magnitude \(6\) is the area of the \(2\times3\) rectangle they span.

quiz.q: By the right-hand rule, \( (1,0,0)\times(0,1,0) \) points in the direction:
quiz.solution: \( \hat{\imath}\times\hat{\jmath}=\hat{k} \): fingers from \(x\) to \(y\), thumb points along \(+z\).

quiz.q: The unit vector in the direction of \( (1,2,2) \) is:
A normal vector to the plane \( 2x-y+2z=3 \) is:
quiz.solution: The coefficients of \(x,y,z\) are the components of the normal: \( \mathbf{n}=(2,-1,2) \).

quiz.q: Which product would you use to find the torque of a force about a pivot?
quiz.solution: Moment is \( \mathbf{M}=\mathbf{r}\times\mathbf{F} \): it keeps the perpendicular part and gives the axis of the twist.

1.kuwait:
On a fabrication floor, a technician tightens a large flange bolt with a \(0.5\)-m breaker bar, pushing with about \(200\) N at the very end, square to the bar. The moment delivered to the bolt is \( M=rF=0.5\times200=100 \) N·m — the exact cross-product calculation of Problem 2. If the same push is applied at an angle, only the perpendicular component \( F\sin\theta \) counts, which is why a torque wrench is always pulled at right angles to its handle: any lean wastes effort into a useless along-bar dot-product component that does no turning.

The dot product runs the other side of the same job. When a crane hoists a skid up a ramp, the useful work raising it is \( \mathbf{F}\cdot\mathbf{d} \) — the force resolved along the actual displacement. Estimating this correctly is what sizes the winch motor: counting the full cable tension instead of its along-track projection over-specifies the drive and wastes capital. Two products, two everyday plant decisions — the twist you can apply, and the work you must pay for.

2.lecture:
§1 · Rates when there is more than one variable

Most engineering quantities depend on several inputs at once: the deflection of a beam depends on load and length; the efficiency of a cycle depends on two temperatures; the drag on a body depends on speed and area. A function of several variables \( f(x,y) \) has no single derivative, because "the rate of change" now depends on which input you vary. The device that answers the question one input at a time is the partial derivative: \( \partial f/\partial x \) is the ordinary derivative of \( f \) with respect to \( x \) while every other variable is held constant. That holding-constant is the whole idea — it isolates the sensitivity to one input, which is exactly what a designer needs when asking "if I change only the wall thickness, how much does the stress move?"

§2 · Computing partials is one-variable calculus, repeated

No new differentiation rules are needed. To get \( \partial f/\partial x \), treat \( y \) as a frozen number and differentiate as usual. For \( f(x,y)=x^2y+3y \): holding \( y \) fixed, \( \partial f/\partial x=2xy \); holding \( x \) fixed, \( \partial f/\partial y=x^2+3 \). Evaluated at the point \( (2,1) \) these are \( f_x=2(2)(1)=4 \) and \( f_y=2^2+3=7 \). The two numbers say the surface climbs at rate \(4\) if you step in the \(x\)-direction and rate \(7\) if you step in the \(y\)-direction from that point. Each partial is a slope of a slice — the curve you get by cutting the surface with a plane that keeps the other variable fixed.

Definition — partial derivative

\[ \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}=\lim_{h\to0}\frac{f(x+h,y)-f(x,y)}{h} \]

Differentiate with respect to one variable, treating all others as constants. It is the sensitivity of \( f \) to that one input.

§3 · The gradient: assembling the partials into a direction

Collect the partials into a vector and something powerful appears — the gradient \( \nabla f=(f_x,\,f_y) \). It is not just bookkeeping: the gradient points in the direction of steepest ascent of the surface, and its length \( |\nabla f| \) is the maximum rate of climb. At \( (2,1) \) above, \( \nabla f=(4,7) \) and \( |\nabla f|=\sqrt{4^2+7^2}=\sqrt{65}\approx8.06 \) — so the fastest uphill direction there is along \( (4,7) \), climbing at rate \(8.06\), steeper than either axis-aligned slice alone. Crucially, the gradient is always perpendicular to the level curves (the contours of constant \( f \)), which is why water runs straight down a hillside across the contour lines rather than along them.

Key idea — the gradient

\[ \nabla f=\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial x},\,\frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\right);\qquad |\nabla f|=\text{steepest rate},\quad \nabla f\perp\text{level curves} \]

§4 · Second and mixed partials

Partials can be taken again. Differentiating \( f_x \) with respect to \( x \) gives \( f_{xx} \), the curvature along \( x \); differentiating \( f_x \) with respect to \( y \) gives the mixed partial \( f_{xy} \). A remarkable fact — Clairaut's theorem — is that for any smooth function the order does not matter: \( f_{xy}=f_{yx} \). Check it on \( f=x^2y+3y \): from \( f_x=2xy \), \( f_{xy}=2x \); from \( f_y=x^2+3 \), \( f_{yx}=2x \). They agree, \(=4\) at \( x=2 \). This symmetry is not a curiosity — it underlies the exactness conditions for differential equations, the existence of potential functions, and the reciprocity relations of thermodynamics, all of which are Clairaut's theorem wearing different clothes.

§5 · Linear approximation and error propagation

The partials give the best flat approximation to a surface near a point: \( \Delta f\approx f_x\,\Delta x+f_y\,\Delta y \). This is the engineer's sensitivity and error-propagation formula in one line. Near \( (2,1) \), a change \( \Delta x=0.1,\ \Delta y=0.05 \) predicts \( \Delta f\approx4(0.1)+7(0.05)=0.75 \); the exact change is \( f(2.1,1.05)-f(2,1)=0.7805 \), so the linear estimate is right to a few percent for a step this size. When \( x \) and \( y \) are measured quantities with uncertainties, this same formula tells you how their errors combine into the uncertainty of \( f \) — the partial derivatives are precisely the weights each input's error carries into the result.

Key formula — sensitivity / error

\[ \Delta f\approx \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\,\Delta x+\frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\,\Delta y \]

The partials are the weights that turn input changes (or measurement errors) into the change (or uncertainty) of \( f \).

§6 · The working method

Faced with a multivariable function, the disciplined order is: (1) identify the variables and which are held constant for each question; (2) take the partials one variable at a time, reusing ordinary calculus; (3) assemble \( \nabla f \) when a direction or a maximum rate is wanted, and read \( |\nabla f| \) for the steepest slope; (4) for a small-change or uncertainty question, apply the linear formula \( \Delta f\approx f_x\Delta x+f_y\Delta y \) rather than recomputing \( f \) twice. This toolkit — partials, gradient, linearization — is the entire language in which heat transfer writes temperature fields, in which optimization finds minima by setting \( \nabla f=\mathbf{0} \), and in which control theory measures sensitivity. It is multivariable calculus reduced to a small set of moves you will use for the next three years.

§7 · The rate in any direction

The two partials give the slopes along the axes, but the slope in an arbitrary direction follows immediately from the gradient: the directional derivative in a unit direction \( \hat{\mathbf{u}} \) is \( D_{\hat{\mathbf{u}}}f=\nabla f\cdot\hat{\mathbf{u}} \), a dot product of the gradient with the way you intend to step. This single formula contains everything in the lesson — it is largest when \( \hat{\mathbf{u}} \) aligns with \( \nabla f \) (steepest ascent, value \( |\nabla f| \)), zero when \( \hat{\mathbf{u}} \) is perpendicular to \( \nabla f \) (moving along a level curve, no change), and negative downhill. At \( (2,1) \) with \( \nabla f=(4,7) \), stepping along \( \hat{\mathbf{u}}=(1,0) \) gives \( D_{\hat{\mathbf{u}}}f=4 \), recovering \( f_x \) as the special case it is. The gradient plus one dot product answers the rate-of-change question in every direction at once.
2.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Single-variable differentiation — every partial is one of these.
  • Vectors and magnitude (Lesson 1) — the gradient is a vector.
  • The idea of a slope — extended to slices of a surface.

Toolkit — laws, formulas, and constants

QuantityFormulaWhen to use
Partial derivative\( f_x=\partial f/\partial x \) (hold \(y\) fixed)Sensitivity to one input.
Gradient\( \nabla f=(f_x,f_y) \)Direction of steepest ascent.
Steepest rate\( |\nabla f| \)Maximum slope at a point.
Level curve\( f(x,y)=c \)Contours; \( \nabla f \perp \) them.
Mixed partial (Clairaut)\( f_{xy}=f_{yx} \)Order of differentiation is free (smooth \(f\)).

Stationary point\( \nabla f=\mathbf{0} \)Candidate maxima/minima (optimization).

quiz.q:
Partials and gradient

Problem. For \( f(x,y)=x^2y+3y \), find \( f_x \) and \( f_y \), then the gradient and its magnitude at \( (2,1) \).
quiz.solution: Solution. Holding \(y\) fixed, \( f_x=2xy \); holding \(x\) fixed, \( f_y=x^2+3 \). At \( (2,1) \): \( f_x=4 \), \( f_y=7 \). So \( \nabla f=(4,7) \) and \( |\nabla f|=\sqrt{4^2+7^2}=\sqrt{65}\approx8.06 \). The steepest climb at that point is along \( (4,7) \).

quiz.q:
Clairaut's theorem

Problem. Verify \( f_{xy}=f_{yx} \) for \( f=x^2y+3y \).
quiz.solution: Solution. From \( f_x=2xy \), differentiate in \(y\): \( f_{xy}=2x \). From \( f_y=x^2+3 \), differentiate in \(x\): \( f_{yx}=2x \). They are equal (\(=4\) at \(x=2\)), as Clairaut's theorem guarantees for a smooth function.

quiz.q:
Error propagation

Problem. Near \( (2,1) \), estimate the change in \( f=x^2y+3y \) when \( x \) rises by \(0.1\) and \( y \) by \(0.05\), using the linear approximation. Compare with the exact change.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( \Delta f\approx f_x\Delta x+f_y\Delta y=4(0.1)+7(0.05)=0.4+0.35=0.75 \). Exact: \( f(2.1,1.05)-f(2,1)=7.7805-7=0.7805 \). The linear estimate \(0.75\) is within about 4% — accurate for a small step, and the basis of uncertainty analysis.

quiz.q: \( \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}(x^2y+3y) \) equals:
quiz.solution: Treat \(y\) as constant: \( \partial(x^2y)/\partial x=2xy \) and \( \partial(3y)/\partial x=0 \).

quiz.q: \( \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y}(x^2y+3y) \) equals:
quiz.solution: Treat \(x\) as constant: \( \partial(x^2y)/\partial y=x^2 \) and \( \partial(3y)/\partial y=3 \).

quiz.q: The gradient \( \nabla f \) points in the direction of:
quiz.solution: \( \nabla f \) points uphill fastest; \( -\nabla f \) is steepest descent, and it is perpendicular to the level curves.

quiz.q: For \( g=x^2+y^2 \), the magnitude \( |\nabla g| \) at \( (1,2) \) is:
Clairaut's theorem states that for a smooth function:
quiz.solution: The mixed second partials are equal regardless of the order of differentiation.

quiz.q: The partial derivative \( \partial f/\partial x \) is computed by:
quiz.solution: By definition every other variable is treated as a constant while differentiating with respect to \(x\).

quiz.q: The linear change estimate \( \Delta f \) for \( f=x^2y+3y \) at \( (2,1) \) with \( \Delta x=0.1,\ \Delta y=0.05 \) is:
quiz.solution: \( \Delta f\approx f_x\Delta x+f_y\Delta y=4(0.1)+7(0.05)=0.75 \).

quiz.q: To find candidate maxima and minima of \( f(x,y) \), one solves:
quiz.solution: At an interior extremum every partial vanishes, so \( \nabla f=\mathbf{0} \) — the multivariable analogue of \( f'(x)=0 \).

2.kuwait:
A process engineer sizing a heat exchanger works with a duty that depends on several inputs at once — flow rate, inlet temperature, and area. Asking "how much does the duty move if only the cooling-water flow rises by 5%?" is exactly a partial derivative: freeze every other variable and read the sensitivity. On the plant floor this decides which input is worth controlling tightly and which can drift; the variable with the largest partial derivative is the one the control loop should watch, because its errors dominate the linear error-propagation sum \( \Delta f\approx f_x\Delta x+f_y\Delta y \).

The gradient's "steepest ascent" reading is just as practical. In a furnace or a cast billet, heat flows down the steepest temperature drop — along \( -\nabla T \), straight across the isotherms — never along a line of constant temperature. A maintenance thermographer reading a heat map is really reading level curves, and the hot-spot risk is wherever those contours crowd together, because crowded contours mean a large \( |\nabla T| \) and a steep, stressful thermal gradient.

3.lecture:
§1 · The total differential

The partials of the last lesson combine into the total differential, the exact statement of how a function changes when all its inputs move together: \( df=f_x\,dx+f_y\,dy \). It is the linear approximation written as an identity between infinitesimals, and it is the workhorse for everything that follows — chain rules, error analysis, and the exactness tests of differential equations all start here. Read it plainly: the change in \( f \) is the sum of each input's change times how strongly \( f \) responds to that input. For a cylinder volume \( V=\pi r^2h \), \( dV=2\pi r h\,dr+\pi r^2\,dh \) — the first term is the volume gained by growing the radius, the second by growing the height.

§2 · The chain rule along one variable

When \( x \) and \( y \) are themselves functions of a single driver \( t \), the function \( f(x,y) \) becomes a function of \( t \) alone, and its rate is the sum over both paths in the tree above: \( \dfrac{df}{dt}=f_x\dfrac{dx}{dt}+f_y\dfrac{dy}{dt} \). Each term is a path from \( f \) down to \( t \); you multiply the links along a path and add the paths. Take \( f=xy \) with \( x=t,\ y=t^2 \): then \( f_x=y=t^2,\ f_y=x=t \), and with \( x'=1,\ y'=2t \), \( \dfrac{df}{dt}=t^2(1)+t(2t)=3t^2 \). This matches differentiating \( f=t\cdot t^2=t^3 \) directly, \(=3t^2\) — a built-in check that the path-sum bookkeeping is right.

Key rule — the chain rule

\[ \frac{df}{dt}=\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\frac{dx}{dt}+\frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\frac{dy}{dt} \]

One term per path from \(f\) to the driver; multiply the links along a path, sum the paths.

§3 · Several independent variables

If the intermediate variables depend on more than one driver — \( x=x(s,t),\ y=y(s,t) \) — the same path-sum gives a partial derivative for each driver, holding the others fixed: \( \dfrac{\partial z}{\partial s}=f_x\dfrac{\partial x}{\partial s}+f_y\dfrac{\partial y}{\partial s} \). For \( z=x^2+y^2 \) with \( x=s+t,\ y=s-t \): \( z_s=2x(1)+2y(1)=2(s+t)+2(s-t)=4s \), and likewise \( z_t=4t \). The tree simply grows another level, but the rule is unchanged: enumerate the paths to the driver in question, and add. This is precisely how a change of coordinates — Cartesian to polar, say — propagates derivatives, which heat transfer and fluid mechanics lean on constantly.

§4 · Error propagation, the everyday payoff

The total differential is the engineer's error-propagation law. If each input carries an uncertainty, a worst-case bound adds the magnitudes: \( |\Delta f|\le|f_x||\Delta x|+|f_y||\Delta y| \). Dividing the cylinder's differential by \( V \) gives the tidy relative form \( \dfrac{dV}{V}=2\dfrac{dr}{r}+\dfrac{dh}{h} \) — the radius counts double because it is squared. With \( dr/r=0.5\% \) and \( dh/h=0.4\% \), the volume is uncertain by \( 2(0.5)+0.4=1.4\% \). But when the errors are independent random scatter rather than systematic, they combine in quadrature — root-sum-square — because unrelated errors partly cancel: \( \dfrac{\sigma_V}{V}=\sqrt{(2\cdot0.5\%)^2+(0.4\%)^2}\approx1.08\% \), smaller than the worst case. Knowing which combination applies is the difference between an honest tolerance and a padded one.

Key formulas — error propagation

Worst case: \( |\Delta f|\le|f_x||\Delta x|+|f_y||\Delta y| \). Independent random: \( \sigma_f=\sqrt{(f_x\sigma_x)^2+(f_y\sigma_y)^2} \).

§5 · The working method

The routine is short. To differentiate a composite, draw the dependency tree, write one product-term per path from the function to the driver, and sum. To propagate uncertainty, take the total differential, divide by \( f \) if a relative error is wanted, and then either add magnitudes (worst case, correlated or unknown errors) or root-sum-square (independent random errors). The recurring engineering lesson is that a squared or cubed variable carries its exponent as a multiplier into the error budget — so the dimension measured least carefully, weighted by its exponent, is usually the one that dominates the tolerance, and the one worth a better instrument.

quiz.q:
Chain rule, one driver

Problem. With \( f=xy \), \( x=t \), \( y=t^2 \), find \( df/dt \) two ways and evaluate at \( t=2 \).
quiz.solution: Solution. Chain rule: \( f_x=y=t^2 \), \( f_y=x=t \), \( x'=1 \), \( y'=2t \), so \( df/dt=t^2(1)+t(2t)=3t^2 \). Directly: \( f=t\cdot t^2=t^3 \Rightarrow df/dt=3t^2 \). At \( t=2 \), \( df/dt=12 \).

quiz.q:
Total differential of a volume

Problem. A cylinder has \( r=2 \) m, \( h=5 \) m. Estimate \( dV \) for \( dr=0.01 \) m, \( dh=0.02 \) m, and give the relative change.

Independent-error quadrature

Problem. For the same cylinder, \( r \) and \( h \) are measured with independent random relative uncertainties \(0.5\%\) and \(0.4\%\). Find the resulting relative uncertainty in \(V\).
quiz.solution: Solution. The radius contributes with weight 2 (it is squared). Independent errors add in quadrature: \( \dfrac{\sigma_V}{V}=\sqrt{(2\cdot0.5\%)^2+(0.4\%)^2}=\sqrt{(1.0\%)^2+(0.4\%)^2}=\sqrt{1.16}\,\%\approx1.08\% \) — below the \(1.4\%\) worst case, because unrelated errors partly cancel.

quiz.q: The total differential of \( f(x,y) \) is:
quiz.solution: Each input's change times the sensitivity to it, summed: \( df=f_x\,dx+f_y\,dy \).

quiz.q: For \( f(x(t),y(t)) \), the chain rule gives \( df/dt= \):
quiz.solution: Sum one product per path from \(f\) to \(t\): \( f_x\frac{dx}{dt}+f_y\frac{dy}{dt} \).

quiz.q: For a cylinder \( V=\pi r^2h \), the relative error is:
quiz.solution: The exponent of each variable becomes its weight: \(r\) is squared (weight 2), \(h\) is linear (weight 1).

quiz.q: Independent random measurement errors combine by:
quiz.solution: Unrelated errors partly cancel, so they add in quadrature: \( \sigma_f=\sqrt{\sum (f_{x_i}\sigma_{x_i})^2} \).

quiz.q: For \( z=x^2+y^2 \) with \( x=s+t,\ y=s-t \), \( \partial z/\partial s \) equals:
The worst-case error bound for \( f(x,y) \) uses:
quiz.solution: The worst case assumes errors reinforce, so magnitudes are summed: \( |\Delta f|\le|f_x||\Delta x|+|f_y||\Delta y| \).

quiz.q: In a squared quantity, a 1% error in the base variable produces about:
quiz.solution: The exponent multiplies the relative error: \( d(x^2)/x^2=2\,dx/x \), so 1% becomes 2%.

A metrology technician certifying a machined shaft measures its diameter and length to compute volume or mass. Because volume goes as the diameter squared, the total-differential rule \( dV/V=2\,dd/d+dL/L \) warns that any error in the diameter is doubled in the result — so the caliper on the diameter must be twice as trustworthy as the tape on the length to reach the same volume tolerance. This is why inspection plans spend their tightest gauges on the dimensions that enter with the highest exponent; it is error propagation setting the measurement budget, not guesswork.

The quadrature rule decides how a stack of independent tolerances accumulates. When several machined parts assemble in series, their independent gap errors do not simply add to a worst case — they combine root-sum-square, so a five-part stack of \(\pm0.1\) mm features scatters by about \( \sqrt5\times0.1\approx0.22 \) mm, not \(0.5\) mm. Designing to the honest statistical stack rather than the pessimistic arithmetic one is what keeps assemblies both reliable and affordable.

4.lecture:
§1 · Integrating over a region

A single integral accumulates along a line; a double integral \( \iint_R f\,dA \) accumulates over a two-dimensional region \( R \), summing the value of \( f \) on every infinitesimal tile \( dA=dx\,dy \). With \( f=1 \) it returns the plain area of \( R \); with \( f \) a height it returns a volume; with \( f \) a density it returns a mass. The practical key is Fubini's theorem: a double integral is computed as an iterated integral, one variable then the other, and for a well-behaved region the order may be swapped. Over a rectangle the two directions even separate: \( \iint_{[0,2]\times[0,3]}xy\,dA=\Big(\int_0^2 x\,dx\Big)\Big(\int_0^3 y\,dy\Big)=(2)(4.5)=9 \).

§2 · Mass and centroid

Give the region a density \( \rho \) and the same integral computes physical properties of a plate or lamina. The mass is \( m=\iint_R \rho\,dA \), and the centroid — the balance point — is the density-weighted average position, \( \bar{x}=\dfrac{1}{m}\iint_R x\,\rho\,dA \), with \( \bar{y} \) similar. For a uniform rectangle \( [0,2]\times[0,3] \) the centroid sits at its geometric centre \( (1,\,1.5) \), as symmetry demands. For a right triangle with legs \( b,h \) the area is \( bh/2 \) and the centroid lands at \( (b/3,\,h/3) \) — one-third of the way from the right-angle along each leg, a result worth memorising because triangular cross-sections are everywhere in structures.

Key formulas — mass & centroid

\[ m=\iint_R \rho\,dA,\qquad \bar{x}=\frac{1}{m}\iint_R x\,\rho\,dA,\qquad \bar{y}=\frac{1}{m}\iint_R y\,\rho\,dA \]

§3 · Moment of inertia

Weight the area by the square of distance from an axis and the integral becomes the second moment of area (area moment of inertia) — the geometric quantity that sets a beam's stiffness and a shaft's resistance to twist. About its base, a rectangle of width \( b \) and height \( h \) has \( I=\iint y^2\,dA=\int_0^h\!\int_0^b y^2\,dx\,dy=\dfrac{bh^3}{3} \); for \( b=3,\ h=2 \) this is \( 8 \). About the centroidal axis the value drops to \( \dfrac{bh^3}{12}=2 \). The two are linked by the parallel-axis theorem \( I=I_c+Ad^2 \): here \( I_c+Ad^2=2+6(1)^2=8 \), recovering the base value. The cubic dependence on \( h \) is why depth, far more than width, buys bending stiffness.

Key results — second moment of area

Rectangle: \( I_{\text{base}}=\dfrac{bh^3}{3},\quad I_{\text{centroid}}=\dfrac{bh^3}{12} \). Parallel axis: \( I=I_c+Ad^2 \).

§4 · Triple integrals and the method

Adding a third direction gives the triple integral \( \iiint_V f\,dV \) over a solid: with \( f=1 \) it is the volume, with \( f=\rho \) the mass, with \( f=\rho\,r^2 \) the mass moment of inertia of a real three-dimensional part. A box \( [0,1]\times[0,2]\times[0,3] \) has volume \( 1\cdot2\cdot3=6 \). The method for every case is one habit: decide what quantity sits inside the integral (\(1\), a height, a density, or a squared distance), set the limits by sweeping the region in a sensible order, integrate one variable at a time holding the rest fixed, and check the units of the answer. Choosing the integration order to match the region — and, in the next lesson, choosing the right coordinates — is what turns a hard region into an easy calculation.

quiz.q:
A separable double integral

Problem. Evaluate \( \displaystyle\int_0^3\!\!\int_0^2 xy\,dx\,dy \).
quiz.solution: Solution. Inner: \( \int_0^2 xy\,dx=y\big[\tfrac{x^2}{2}\big]_0^2=2y \). Outer: \( \int_0^3 2y\,dy=\big[y^2\big]_0^3=9 \). Over a rectangle the factors separate: \( (\int_0^2 x\,dx)(\int_0^3 y\,dy)=(2)(4.5)=9 \).

quiz.q:
Second moment of area

Problem. A rectangle has width \( b=3 \), height \( h=2 \). Find its second moment of area about the base and about the centroid, and check with the parallel-axis theorem.
quiz.solution: Solution. About the base \( I=\iint y^2\,dA=\dfrac{bh^3}{3}=\dfrac{3\cdot8}{3}=8 \). About the centroid \( I_c=\dfrac{bh^3}{12}=\dfrac{24}{12}=2 \). Check: \( A=6 \), \( d=h/2=1 \), so \( I_c+Ad^2=2+6(1)=8 \) ✓.

quiz.q:
Centroid of a triangle

Problem. Find the area and centroid of a right triangle with legs \( b=3 \) (along \(x\)) and \( h=6 \) (along \(y\)), right angle at the origin.
quiz.solution: Solution. Area \( =\tfrac12 bh=\tfrac12(3)(6)=9 \). The centroid of a triangle is one-third of the way from the right angle along each leg: \( \bar x=b/3=1 \), \( \bar y=h/3=2 \). So \( (\bar x,\bar y)=(1,2) \).

quiz.q: \( \iint_R dA \) (with \( f=1 \)) gives:
quiz.solution: Summing \(1\) over every tile \(dA\) accumulates the region's area.

quiz.q: \( \int_0^3\!\int_0^2 xy\,dx\,dy \) equals:
Fubini's theorem lets you:
choices: compute a double integral as two single integrals
quiz.solution: It reduces \( \iint f\,dA \) to an iterated integral, and (for nice regions) the order may be swapped.

quiz.q: The mass of a plate of density \( \rho \) is:
quiz.solution: Mass is density accumulated over the area: \( m=\iint_R\rho\,dA \).

quiz.q: The second moment of area of a rectangle about its base is:
quiz.solution: \( I_{\text{base}}=\iint y^2\,dA=\frac{bh^3}{3} \); the centroidal value is \( \frac{bh^3}{12} \).

quiz.q: The parallel-axis theorem states:
quiz.solution: Moving off the centroid adds \( Ad^2 \): \( I=I_c+Ad^2 \).

quiz.q: The centroid of a right triangle with legs \(b,h\) (right angle at origin) is at:
quiz.solution: A triangle's centroid is one-third from the right angle along each leg: \( (b/3,h/3) \).

quiz.q: Doubling a beam's depth \(h\) (width fixed) multiplies its base second moment \( bh^3/3 \) by:
quiz.solution: \(I\propto h^3\), so \( h\to2h \) gives \( 2^3=8\times \) the stiffness — why depth dominates bending.

4.kuwait:
A structural draftsman selecting a floor beam relies directly on the second moment of area: a beam's stiffness scales as \( bh^3/12 \), so the depth \(h\) enters cubed while the width \(b\) enters only linearly. Doubling the depth stiffens the beam eightfold; doubling the width merely doubles it. That single integral result is why rolled sections are deep and thin rather than square, and why an I-beam pushes its material as far from the neutral axis as the geometry allows — the \( y^2 \) weight in \( \iint y^2\,dA \) rewards distance from the axis above all.

The centroid integral does the balancing. When a fabricator lifts an irregular steel plate with a single sling, the lift point must sit over the centroid \( \bar x=\frac1A\iint x\,dA \), or the plate swings and tilts. For composite plates the same rule is applied piecewise — locate each simple piece's centroid, then take the area-weighted average — which is the everyday shop version of evaluating the integral without calculus.

5.lecture:
§1 · Fields: a vector at every point

A vector field \( \mathbf{F}(x,y)=(P,Q) \) assigns a vector to each point of the plane — a picture of flow. The velocity of water in a channel, the force on a charge in space, the heat flux through a wall are all vector fields, and the calculus of this lesson is how you extract physical quantities from them: the work a field does along a path, the flow across a boundary, and the local tendencies to spread or to rotate. Two derived scalars, the divergence and the curl, summarise a field's local behaviour completely, and both are built from the partial derivatives of \( P \) and \( Q \) you already know.

§2 · Line integrals: work and circulation

Integrating a field along a curve \( C \) accumulates the component of \( \mathbf{F} \) that lies along the path: \( \displaystyle\int_C \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{r} \). When \( \mathbf{F} \) is a force this is the work done moving along \( C \); when \( C \) is a closed loop it is the circulation, the net "push around the loop." For a constant field \( \mathbf{F}=(3,4) \) along the straight segment from \( (0,0) \) to \( (2,0) \), only the \(x\)-component acts and \( W=3(2)=6 \). For \( \mathbf{F}=(x,y) \) along the diagonal from \( (0,0) \) to \( (1,1) \), parametrise \( \mathbf{r}=(t,t) \): then \( \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{r}=2t\,dt \) and \( \int_0^1 2t\,dt=1 \). The line integral is just the dot product of Lesson 1, summed continuously along a path.

§3 · Flux: flow across a boundary

Rotate the question ninety degrees and ask how much field crosses a curve rather than runs along it — the flux \( \displaystyle\int_C \mathbf{F}\cdot\mathbf{n}\,ds \), with \( \mathbf{n} \) the outward normal. Flux measures throughput: the water leaving a region, the heat escaping a surface, the current through a loop. Where circulation uses the component along the boundary, flux uses the component through it — the same field, read with the tangential versus the normal direction. These two integrals, circulation and flux, are the complete pair of things you can ask of a field on a boundary, and each connects to a local density in the interior.

§4 · Divergence: sources and sinks

The local density behind flux is the divergence: \( \operatorname{div}\mathbf{F}=\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial x}+\dfrac{\partial Q}{\partial y} \), the net outflow per unit area at a point. A positive divergence marks a source (field spreading outward), a negative one a sink. For \( \mathbf{F}=(x,y) \), \( \operatorname{div}\mathbf{F}=1+1=2 \) everywhere — a uniform source, arrows radiating outward as in the left panel. For the rotation field \( \mathbf{F}=(-y,x) \), \( \operatorname{div}\mathbf{F}=0 \): nothing is created, the flow is incompressible. Zero divergence is exactly the condition that makes a fluid conserve volume, which is why it is the governing equation of incompressible flow.

Key operator — divergence

\[ \operatorname{div}\mathbf{F}=\frac{\partial P}{\partial x}+\frac{\partial Q}{\partial y} \]

Net outflow per area. Positive ⇒ source, negative ⇒ sink, zero ⇒ incompressible.

§5 · Curl: swirl and rotation

The local density behind circulation is the curl: in the plane, \( \operatorname{curl}\mathbf{F}=\dfrac{\partial Q}{\partial x}-\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial y} \), the swirl per unit area. For \( \mathbf{F}=(-y,x) \), \( \operatorname{curl}\mathbf{F}=1-(-1)=2 \): a uniform counter-clockwise rotation, the right panel. For the source field \( \mathbf{F}=(x,y) \), \( \operatorname{curl}\mathbf{F}=0 \) — pure spreading with no rotation. A field with zero curl everywhere is called irrotational, and it is precisely the condition for the field to be a gradient \( \nabla f \) of some potential, tying this lesson back to Lesson 2. Divergence and curl are the two independent things a field can do locally: spread, and spin.

Key operator — curl (2-D)

\[ \operatorname{curl}\mathbf{F}=\frac{\partial Q}{\partial x}-\frac{\partial P}{\partial y} \]

Swirl per area. Zero ⇒ irrotational (a gradient field). Nonzero ⇒ local rotation.

§6 · Green's theorem and the method

The boundary integrals and the interior densities are linked by Green's theorem: the circulation around a closed curve equals the integrated curl inside it, \( \oint_C \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{r}=\iint_R \operatorname{curl}\mathbf{F}\,dA \), and likewise total flux equals integrated divergence. This turns a hard boundary integral into an easy area integral, or vice versa. So the rotation field \( (-y,x) \) circulating around the unit circle gives \( \iint 2\,dA=2\pi \). The working method: to read a field, compute \( \operatorname{div} \) (does it source?) and \( \operatorname{curl} \) (does it spin?); to accumulate work or flow, set up the line or flux integral, and invoke Green's theorem whenever swapping a boundary for an interior makes the arithmetic easier. This is the vector calculus that fluid mechanics and electromagnetism are written in.

Divergence and curl of a source

Problem. For \( \mathbf{F}=(x,y) \), compute the divergence and curl, and describe the field.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( \operatorname{div}\mathbf{F}=\partial_x x+\partial_y y=1+1=2 \) and \( \operatorname{curl}\mathbf{F}=\partial_x y-\partial_y x=0-0=0 \). The field is a uniform source (arrows radiate outward, divergence \(2\)) with no rotation (irrotational).

quiz.q:
Divergence and curl of a rotation

Problem. For \( \mathbf{F}=(-y,x) \), compute the divergence and curl, and describe the field.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( \operatorname{div}\mathbf{F}=\partial_x(-y)+\partial_y(x)=0+0=0 \) and \( \operatorname{curl}\mathbf{F}=\partial_x(x)-\partial_y(-y)=1-(-1)=2 \). The field is incompressible (no source) and rotates counter-clockwise with uniform swirl \(2\).

Problem. Find the work done by \( \mathbf{F}=(x,y) \) along the straight path from \( (0,0) \) to \( (1,1) \).

quiz.q: The curl of the rotation field \( \mathbf{F}=(-y,x) \) is:

quiz.q: Divergence measures a field's:
quiz.solution: \( \operatorname{div}\mathbf{F} \) is outflow per unit area — positive for a source, negative for a sink.

quiz.q: A field with zero curl everywhere is called:
quiz.solution: Zero curl ⇒ irrotational ⇒ the field is \( \nabla f \) for some potential \(f\).

quiz.q: The work done by a force field along a path is the:
quiz.solution: Work accumulates the tangential component of \( \mathbf{F} \) along \(C\): \( \int_C \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{r} \).

quiz.q: The flux of a field across a boundary uses the field's component that is:
quiz.solution: Flux \( \int_C \mathbf{F}\cdot\mathbf{n}\,ds \) uses the outward-normal component — flow through the boundary.

quiz.solution: \( \oint_C \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{r}=\iint_R \operatorname{curl}\mathbf{F}\,dA \) — boundary circulation equals interior curl.

quiz.q: An incompressible flow is characterised by:

5.kuwait:
An HVAC or process engineer balancing a duct network is really enforcing zero divergence: whatever mass of air flows into a junction must flow out, so \( \operatorname{div}\mathbf{F}=0 \) at every node. When a survey finds a node where the measured inflow and outflow do not match — a nonzero divergence — it is a physical alarm: an unmetered leak, a blocked branch, or a faulty sensor. The mathematics that looks abstract on the page is, on the plant floor, the mass balance an instrument technician chases down.

Curl shows up wherever rotation is diagnosed. In a stirred mixing vessel or a pump volute, the swirl of the flow is a curl field, and too much of it near a pump inlet promotes the vortexing that starves the pump and invites cavitation. Reading a flow map for regions of high curl is how a rotating-equipment engineer spots the recirculation zones that wear impellers — the same operator that, in the next courses, becomes the vorticity of fluid mechanics and the rotation of a rigid body in dynamics.

6.lecture:
§1 · A system is one matrix equation

A set of linear equations is a single object. The pair \( 2x+y=5,\ x+3y=10 \) is the matrix equation \( A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b} \) with

\[ A=\begin{pmatrix}2&1\\1&3\end{pmatrix}. \]

with unknown vector \( \mathbf{x}=(x,\,y)^\top \) and right-hand side \( \mathbf{b}=(5,\,10)^\top \). The coefficients become the matrix, the unknowns a vector, the right-hand sides another vector. This packaging is what lets a system of two equations and one of two hundred be reasoned about with the same tools — the truss with its joint-equilibrium equations, the circuit with its node equations, the mixing plant with its mass balances all arrive as \( A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b} \). The product \( A\mathbf{x} \) dots each row of \(A\) into \(\mathbf{x}\); checking the solution \( (1,3) \) gives \( 2(1)+1(3)=5 \) and \( 1(1)+3(3)=10 \), reproducing \(\mathbf{b}\).

§2 · Determinant, inverse, and a unique answer

For a \( 2\times2 \) matrix the determinant \( \det A=ad-bc \) decides solvability in one number: nonzero means exactly one solution exists for every \(\mathbf{b}\); zero means the matrix is singular and the system has either none or infinitely many. Here \( \det A=2(3)-1(1)=5\neq0 \). When it is nonzero the inverse solves the system in closed form as \( \mathbf{x}=A^{-1}\mathbf{b} \) (for a \(2\times2\), \( A^{-1}=\tfrac{1}{ad-bc} \) times the swap-and-negate of \(A\)), so here

\[ A^{-1}=\frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix}3&-1\\-1&2\end{pmatrix}. \]

and multiplying by \( \mathbf{b}=(5,10) \) returns \( (1,3) \). For large systems the inverse is too expensive; the method that scales is elimination.

§3 · Gaussian elimination

Gaussian elimination uses three solution-preserving row operations — swap two rows, scale a row, add a multiple of one row to another — to carve the system into an upper-triangular staircase, then reads the unknowns by back-substitution from the bottom. Solve the system

\[ \begin{cases} x+y+z=6\\ 2x+y-z=1\\ x-y+2z=5 \end{cases} \]

Eliminating downward gives \( 7z=21 \Rightarrow z=3 \), then \( -y-3z=-11\Rightarrow y=2 \), then \( x+2+3=6\Rightarrow x=1 \). The solution \( (1,2,3) \) checks in all three equations. This single algorithm is what every computational solver, and every matrix inversion and determinant, is built on.

§4 · Singularity is a physical warning

The nonzero leading entries elimination produces are the pivots, and their count is the rank — the number of genuinely independent equations. Three pivots in a \( 3\times3 \) system mean a unique solution; a missing pivot means a row collapsed to \( 0=0 \) (a redundant equation, infinitely many solutions) or \( 0=c \) (a contradiction, none). A matrix whose rows are \( (1,2) \) and \( (2,4) \) has \( \det=1(4)-2(2)=0 \) and rank 1 — the second row is exactly twice the first. In engineering this is not mere algebra: a singular stiffness matrix means a structure that is a mechanism, free to move without resistance; a singular circuit matrix means an under-determined network. Singularity is the mathematics warning you that the physical system is not properly constrained.

Key test — determinant & rank

\( \det A\neq0 \Rightarrow \) unique solution (full rank). \( \det A=0 \Rightarrow \) singular: a mechanism / under-constrained system, no unique answer.

§5 · The working method

To solve any linear system: (1) write it as \( A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b} \); (2) for a small system compute \( \det A \) — if zero, stop and diagnose the physical under-constraint; (3) reduce by elimination to a triangle, taking the largest available pivot at each step for numerical stability, and back-substitute; (4) count the pivots to confirm the rank and therefore that the answer is unique. Reading a system as one matrix, and reading a zero determinant as a physical alarm rather than an arithmetic dead end, is the habit the eigenvalue lesson and all of later structural and control analysis are built upon.

quiz.q:
Determinant and solution

Problem. For \( A \) with rows \( (2,1) \) and \( (1,3) \), and \( \mathbf{b}=(5,10)^\top \), find \( \det A \) and solve \( A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b} \).
quiz.solution: Solution. \( \det A=2(3)-1(1)=5\neq0 \), so a unique solution exists. By Cramer, \( x=\dfrac{5\cdot3-1\cdot10}{5}=\dfrac{15-10}{5}=1 \) and \( y=\dfrac{2\cdot10-1\cdot5}{5}=\dfrac{20-5}{5}=3 \). Check: \( 2(1)+3=5 \), \( 1+3(3)=10 \) ✓.

quiz.q:
3×3 by elimination

Problem. Solve the system:

\[ \begin{cases} x+y+z=6\\ 2x+y-z=1\\ x-y+2z=5 \end{cases} \]
quiz.solution: Solution. R2−2R1: \( -y-3z=-11 \). R3−R1: \( -2y+z=-1 \). Eliminate \(y\): \( 7z=21\Rightarrow z=3 \). Back-substitute: \( -y-9=-11\Rightarrow y=2 \); \( x+2+3=6\Rightarrow x=1 \). Solution \( (1,2,3) \), verified in all three equations (6, 1, 5).

quiz.q:
Singularity as a warning

Problem. Show that the matrix with rows \( (1,2) \) and \( (2,4) \) is singular and say what that means physically.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( \det=1(4)-2(2)=0 \), so the matrix is singular (rank 1 — the second row is twice the first). The two equations are the same line: no unique solution. Physically, a singular stiffness matrix is a mechanism — a structure free to move without resistance, i.e. improperly constrained.

quiz.q: The determinant of the matrix with rows \( (2,1) \) and \( (1,3) \) equals:
\( A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b} \) has a unique solution for every \(\mathbf{b}\) when:
quiz.solution: A nonzero determinant means \(A\) is invertible, giving \( \mathbf{x}=A^{-1}\mathbf{b} \).

quiz.q: The number of pivots from elimination is the matrix's:
quiz.solution: Pivots count the independent rows — that is the rank.

quiz.q: A singular stiffness matrix physically indicates:
quiz.solution: \( \det=0 \) means the structure can move without resistance — a mechanism, not properly constrained.

quiz.q: Which is NOT a valid row operation?
choices: scaling a row by a nonzero constant
adding a multiple of one row to another
quiz.solution: Only swap, scale, and add-a-multiple preserve the solution set.

quiz.q:
The solution of the system

\[ \begin{cases} x+y+z=6\\ 2x+y-z=1\\ x-y+2z=5 \end{cases} \]

is:
quiz.solution: Elimination gives \( z=3,\ y=2,\ x=1 \).

quiz.q: The inverse of the matrix with rows \( (2,1) \) and \( (1,3) \) is:
quiz.solution: \( A^{-1}=\frac{1}{\det A} \) times the swap-and-negate of \(A\): \( \frac15 \) times the matrix with rows \( (3,-1) \) and \( (-1,2) \).

quiz.q: Partial pivoting (choosing the largest available pivot) improves:
quiz.solution: Dividing by the largest available entry avoids amplifying round-off from tiny pivots.

6.kuwait:
A structural engineer analysing a steel frame assembles a stiffness matrix \(K\) and solves \( K\mathbf{u}=\mathbf{F} \) for the joint displacements under load. The determinant is checked first for a reason: if \( \det K=0 \), the software returns no unique displacement, and that is not a numerical glitch — it means the frame, as modelled, is a mechanism that would collapse or drift because a support or brace is missing. The zero determinant on the screen is the algebra reporting a real buildability defect before any steel is cut.

The same \( A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b} \) governs a plant's mass balances and a building's electrical load flow: nodes conserve what enters and leaves, producing one equation per node. Gaussian elimination is exactly what the balancing software runs, and a near-singular system — rows almost proportional — warns that two measurements are redundant or two loops are effectively the same, so the network is being over-described and cannot be pinned down uniquely.

7.lecture:
§1 · The directions a matrix leaves alone

When a matrix \(A\) acts on a vector it generally changes both its length and its direction. A special few vectors are only scaled — their direction survives untouched. These are the eigenvectors, and the scale factor is the eigenvalue \( \lambda \), defined by \( A\mathbf{v}=\lambda\mathbf{v} \) with \( \mathbf{v}\neq\mathbf{0} \). Along an eigenvector the complicated action of \(A\) collapses to plain multiplication by a number. Finding these directions is finding the natural axes of the transformation — the coordinates in which the matrix does nothing but stretch — and it is why eigenvalues govern the natural frequencies of structures, the stability of systems, and the principal directions of stress.

§2 · The characteristic equation

Rearrange \( A\mathbf{v}=\lambda\mathbf{v} \) into \( (A-\lambda I)\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{0} \). A nonzero \( \mathbf{v} \) exists only if \( A-\lambda I \) is singular, i.e. \( \det(A-\lambda I)=0 \) — the characteristic equation. For any \( 2\times2 \) matrix it takes the compact form

\[ \lambda^2-(\operatorname{tr}A)\,\lambda+\det A=0, \]

using the trace (sum of the diagonal) and the determinant. For the matrix with rows \( (4,1) \) and \( (2,3) \), \( \operatorname{tr}A=7 \) and \( \det A=10 \), so \( \lambda^2-7\lambda+10=0 \), giving \( \lambda=2 \) and \( \lambda=5 \). Two checks are built in: the eigenvalues sum to the trace (\(2+5=7\)) and multiply to the determinant (\(2\times5=10\)).

§3 · The eigenvectors

Each eigenvalue has its own direction, found by solving \( (A-\lambda I)\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{0} \). For the matrix with rows \( (0,1) \) and \( (-2,-3) \), the characteristic equation is \( \lambda^2+3\lambda+2=0 \), so \( \lambda=-1,-2 \). For \( \lambda=-1 \), the equation \( (A+I)\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{0} \) reduces to \( v_1+v_2=0 \), giving the direction \( \mathbf{v}=(1,-1) \). Check: \( A(1,-1)^\top=(-1,\,1)=-1\cdot(1,-1) \) ✓. Eigenvectors are directions, not unique vectors — any nonzero multiple is equally an eigenvector — because the defining relation scales freely. What matters is the line the eigenvector picks out.

§4 · Why engineers compute them

The eigenvalues are the natural rates and frequencies of a system. A coupled system of differential equations \( \mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x} \) — the multi-variable successor to a single second-order equation — has solutions built from \( e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v} \), one exponential mode per eigenvalue evolving along its eigenvector. The sign of each eigenvalue's real part then decides stability at a glance: all negative means every mode decays and the system is stable; any positive means a mode grows and it is unstable; imaginary parts set oscillation frequencies. In a structure these same eigenvectors become the mode shapes — the patterns in which a machine or building naturally vibrates — which the Vibrations course reads directly off the matrix.

Key results — the eigenproblem

\( A\mathbf{v}=\lambda\mathbf{v} \); solve \( \det(A-\lambda I)=0 \). For \(2\times2\): \( \lambda^2-(\operatorname{tr}A)\lambda+\det A=0 \). Stability of \( \mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x} \): all \( \operatorname{Re}\lambda<0 \).

quiz.q:
Eigenvalues by the shortcut

Problem. Find the eigenvalues of the matrix with rows \( (4,1) \) and \( (2,3) \).
quiz.solution: Solution. \( \operatorname{tr}A=4+3=7 \), \( \det A=4(3)-1(2)=10 \). The characteristic equation is \( \lambda^2-7\lambda+10=0=(\lambda-2)(\lambda-5) \), so \( \lambda=2,5 \). Checks: sum \(2+5=7=\operatorname{tr}\) ✓, product \(2\cdot5=10=\det\) ✓.

quiz.q:
An eigenvector

Problem. The matrix with rows \( (0,1) \) and \( (-2,-3) \) has eigenvalues \(-1,-2\). Find an eigenvector for \( \lambda=-1 \).
quiz.solution: Solution. \( A-\lambda I=A+I \) has rows \( (1,1) \) and \( (-2,-2) \). The equation \( v_1+v_2=0 \) gives \( \mathbf{v}=(1,-1) \). Verify: \( A(1,-1)^\top=(-1,\,1)=-1\cdot(1,-1) \) ✓. Any nonzero multiple works.

quiz.q:
Stability of a system

Problem. The system \( \mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x} \) has \(A\) with rows \( (0,1) \) and \( (-2,-3) \). Is it stable?
quiz.solution: Solution. The eigenvalues solve \( \lambda^2+3\lambda+2=0 \), giving \( \lambda=-1,-2 \). Both are negative, so every mode \( e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v} \) decays and the system is stable. (This is the damped oscillator \(m=1,c=3,k=2\) written as a first-order system.)

quiz.q: An eigenvector \( \mathbf{v} \) of \(A\) satisfies:
quiz.solution: By definition \( A\mathbf{v}=\lambda\mathbf{v} \): the matrix only scales the eigenvector.

quiz.q: For a \(2\times2\) matrix the characteristic equation is:
quiz.solution: \( \det(A-\lambda I)=0 \) expands to \( \lambda^2-(\operatorname{tr}A)\lambda+\det A=0 \).

quiz.q: The eigenvalues of the matrix with rows \( (4,1) \) and \( (2,3) \) are:
quiz.solution: \( \lambda^2-7\lambda+10=0\Rightarrow\lambda=2,5 \).

quiz.q: A diagonal matrix \( \operatorname{diag}(2,3) \) has eigenvalues:
quiz.solution: A diagonal matrix's eigenvalues are its diagonal entries: \(2\) and \(3\).

quiz.q: The system \( \mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x} \) is stable when every eigenvalue has:
quiz.solution: Each mode is \( e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v} \); a negative real part makes it decay. All negative ⇒ stable.

quiz.q: The eigenvalues of a \(2\times2\) matrix always sum to its:
quiz.solution: From \( \lambda^2-(\operatorname{tr}A)\lambda+\det A=0 \), roots sum to \( \operatorname{tr}A \) and multiply to \( \det A \).

quiz.q: In a vibrating structure, the eigenvectors of the system become the:
quiz.solution: Each eigenvector is a natural pattern of motion — a mode shape — vibrating at the frequency set by its eigenvalue.

quiz.q: Multiplying an eigenvector by a nonzero constant gives:
quiz.solution: \( A(c\mathbf{v})=c\,A\mathbf{v}=c\lambda\mathbf{v}=\lambda(c\mathbf{v}) \): scaling preserves the eigenvector property and the eigenvalue.

7.kuwait:
A rotating-equipment engineer commissioning a pump skid cares about the machine's natural frequencies, and those are eigenvalues. Modelling the rotor and its supports as a small mass-and-stiffness system produces a matrix whose eigenvalues are the squared natural frequencies and whose eigenvectors are the mode shapes — the patterns in which the machine flexes. If an eigenfrequency sits near the running speed, the mode is excited and vibration spikes; the fix is to shift stiffness or mass to move that eigenvalue away, exactly the calculation of §4 turned into a maintenance decision.

The stability reading is just as concrete. A control loop or a process unit modelled as \( \mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x} \) is stable only if every eigenvalue of \(A\) has a negative real part; a single eigenvalue crossing into the positive half-plane is the mathematical signature of a runaway — a surge, an oscillation that grows instead of settling. Engineers therefore compute the spectrum of \(A\) before trusting a design, because the signs of those numbers decide whether the plant settles or shakes itself apart.

Classify the response

Problem. For \( x''+5x'+6x=0 \), find the discriminant, the roots, and the regime.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( \Delta=c^2-4mk=25-24=1>0 \). Roots: \( r=\dfrac{-5\pm1}{2}=-2,\,-3 \). Two real negative roots ⇒ overdamped: \( x=Ae^{-2t}+Be^{-3t} \), a pure decay with no oscillation.

quiz.q:
Natural frequency and damping

Problem. For \( m=1,\ k=100,\ c=8 \), find \( \omega_n \) and \( \zeta \), and state the regime.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( \omega_n=\sqrt{k/m}=\sqrt{100}=10 \) rad/s. Critical damping \( c_c=2\sqrt{mk}=2\sqrt{100}=20 \), so \( \zeta=c/c_c=8/20=0.4 \). Since \( \zeta<1 \), the system is underdamped — it oscillates as it decays.

quiz.q:
Complex roots

Problem. For \( x''+2x'+5x=0 \), find the roots and the damped frequency.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( \Delta=4-20=-16<0 \). Roots \( r=\dfrac{-2\pm\sqrt{-16}}{2}=-1\pm2i \). Underdamped: decay rate \( \sigma=1 \), damped frequency \( \omega_d=2 \), so \( x=e^{-t}(A\cos2t+B\sin2t) \).

quiz.q: The characteristic equation of \( mx''+cx'+kx=0 \) is:
quiz.solution: Substituting \( x=e^{rt} \) gives \( (mr^2+cr+k)e^{rt}=0 \), so \( mr^2+cr+k=0 \).

quiz.q: The regime is decided by the sign of:
quiz.solution: The discriminant \( \Delta=c^2-4mk \) sorts over/critical/under-damped.

quiz.q: \( \Delta>0 \) (two real roots) gives a response that is:
quiz.solution: Two real negative roots give a sum of decaying exponentials — a non-oscillating, overdamped return.

quiz.q: The natural frequency of a mass–spring system is:
quiz.solution: \( \omega_n=\sqrt{k/m} \) — set by stiffness and mass only.

quiz.q: For \( m=1,k=100,c=8 \), the damping ratio \( \zeta \) is:
Critical damping (\( \Delta=0 \)) is the case that:
quiz.solution: The repeated root gives the quickest non-oscillating return — the boundary between over- and under-damped.

quiz.q: The roots of \( x''+5x'+6x=0 \) are:
An underdamped system (\( \zeta<1 \)) responds by:
quiz.solution: Complex roots give \( e^{-\sigma t}(A\cos\omega_d t+B\sin\omega_d t) \) — ringing that fades.

When a maintenance team tunes a machine mount or a vehicle suspension, they are choosing the damping ratio \( \zeta \). Too little damping (\( \zeta\ll1 \)) and the system rings for a long time after every bump — fatigue and noise; too much (\( \zeta>1 \)) and it responds sluggishly. The sweet spot for most equipment sits a little below critical, giving a fast return with only slight overshoot, which is why suspension and isolator datasheets quote a target \( \zeta \) near \(0.2\)–\(0.7\) rather than raw damper values.

The discriminant test is also a design guard on doors, actuators, and closers. A hydraulic door closer is deliberately set near critical damping so the door shuts quickly but does not slam or bounce — exactly \( \Delta\approx0 \). Reading a system's coefficients and computing \( \Delta \) before building it lets an engineer predict whether the finished device will crawl, snap, or ring, without a single prototype.

quiz.q:
Resonant amplitude

Problem. A system \( m=1,\ k=100,\ c=4 \) is driven by \( F_0=10 \) N at \( \omega=\omega_n \). Find the steady amplitude and compare with the static deflection.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( \omega_n=\sqrt{k/m}=10 \) rad/s. At \( \omega=\omega_n \), \( k-m\omega^2=0 \), so \( X=\dfrac{F_0}{c\omega}=\dfrac{10}{4\times10}=0.25 \) m. Static deflection \( F_0/k=0.1 \) m, so resonance magnifies the response by \( 0.25/0.1=2.5\times \).

quiz.q:
Natural frequency

Problem. A \(4\) kg mass on a spring of stiffness \(10\) kN/m is driven harmonically. At what frequency does resonance occur?
quiz.solution: Solution. Resonance is near \( \omega_n=\sqrt{k/m}=\sqrt{10000/4}=\sqrt{2500}=50 \) rad/s. Driving anywhere near \(50\) rad/s excites the large-amplitude response; the design must keep the operating frequency away from it.

quiz.q:
Effect of damping

Problem. For the system of Problem 1, what happens to the resonant amplitude if the damping \(c\) is doubled to \(8\)?
quiz.solution: Solution. At resonance \( X=F_0/(c\omega) \), inversely proportional to \(c\). Doubling \(c\) from \(4\) to \(8\) halves the amplitude: \( X=10/(8\times10)=0.125 \) m. More damping ⇒ a lower, broader peak.

quiz.q: After the transient dies, a harmonically forced system oscillates at:
quiz.solution: The steady forced response is at the driving frequency \( \omega \), with shifted phase and scaled amplitude.

quiz.q: Resonance in a lightly damped system occurs when \( \omega \) is:
quiz.solution: As \( \omega\to\omega_n \), \( (k-m\omega^2)\to0 \), minimising the denominator and peaking the amplitude.

quiz.q: The amplitude at resonance is approximately:
quiz.solution: At \( \omega=\omega_n \) the stiffness term cancels, leaving \( X=F_0/(c\omega_n) \) — set by damping.

quiz.q: Increasing the damping \(c\) of a resonating system:
quiz.solution: Since \( X_{\text{peak}}\propto1/c \), more damping means a smaller, broader resonance.

quiz.q: The static deflection \( F_0/k \) for \( F_0=10 \) N, \( k=100 \) N/m is:
quiz.solution: \( F_0/k=10/100=0.1 \) m — the zero-frequency limit of \( X(\omega) \).

quiz.q: The two defences against resonance are to:
quiz.solution: Either keep the drive frequency away from \( \omega_n \), or raise damping to cap the peak — those are the only two levers.

quiz.q: A vibration spectrum shows a tall line at a frequency because:
choices: a forcing coincides with a natural frequency (resonance)
quiz.solution: Tall spectral lines mark frequencies where an excitation meets a structural natural frequency — a resonance being driven.

quiz.q: Far above resonance (\( \omega\gg\omega_n \)), the steady response amplitude:
choices: becomes small (the mass cannot keep up)
quiz.solution: The \( m\omega^2 \) term dominates the denominator, so \( X\to0 \): at high drive frequency the inertia cannot follow the forcing.

A rotating-equipment engineer balancing a pump or fan is fighting resonance directly. Residual imbalance forces the machine once per revolution, at exactly the running speed; if that speed sits near a natural frequency \( \omega_n \), the amplitude curve of this lesson peaks and vibration climbs to damaging levels. The standard remedies are the two the mathematics allows — trim-balance and re-rate the speed to detune away from \( \omega_n \), or add a damped mount to cap the peak — and a machine is not accepted into service until its running speed is proven to sit on the flat part of this curve, not the peak.

The analyst's frequency spectrum is \( X(\omega) \) sampled across frequencies, and a growing line at a bearing-defect or gear-mesh frequency is a forcing meeting a resonance — an early warning of a fault. Interpreting those peaks, and knowing that their height is governed by damping, is how a maintenance team schedules a repair before a failure rather than after.

10.lecture:
§1 · What the transform is for

The Laplace transform \( \mathcal{L}\{f\}=F(s)=\displaystyle\int_0^\infty e^{-st}f(t)\,dt \) trades a function of time for a function of a new variable \( s \). Its purpose is not the integral but its consequence: differentiation in \( t \) becomes multiplication in \( s \). A differential equation therefore turns into an ordinary algebraic equation in \( F(s) \), which you solve by rearrangement rather than calculus. It is the same strategic move as the \( e^{rt} \) guess of Lesson 8, generalised — and it pays off most when the forcing is awkward: a switch thrown, a step applied, an impulse struck, all handled by the same bookkeeping.

§2 · The table and the derivative rule

A handful of transforms, plus two rules, cover most problems: \( \mathcal{L}\{1\}=\tfrac1s \), \( \mathcal{L}\{e^{at}\}=\tfrac{1}{s-a} \), \( \mathcal{L}\{t\}=\tfrac{1}{s^2} \), and \( \mathcal{L}\{\sin\omega t\}=\tfrac{\omega}{s^2+\omega^2} \), so \( \mathcal{L}\{\sin4t\}=\tfrac{4}{s^2+16} \). Linearity lets you transform term by term, and the derivative rule is the engine:

\[ \mathcal{L}\{y'\}=sY-y(0),\qquad \mathcal{L}\{y''\}=s^2Y-sy(0)-y'(0). \]

Read what this does: each derivative becomes a power of \( s \), and the initial conditions are folded in automatically at the transform step — no separate constant-fixing stage to forget.

Key rule — transform & solve

Method: transform every term (\( \mathcal{L}\{y'\}=sY-y(0) \)) → solve the algebra for \( Y(s) \) → invert with the table. Differentiation in \(t\) becomes multiplication by \(s\).

§3 · Solving an equation by algebra

The method is three steps: transform, solve for \( Y(s) \), invert. Take \( y'+2y=0,\ y(0)=3 \): transforming gives \( sY-3+2Y=0 \), so \( Y=\tfrac{3}{s+2} \), and the table inverts this to \( y=3e^{-2t} \). A forced example \( y'+3y=6,\ y(0)=0 \): transforming gives \( sY+3Y=\tfrac6s \), so \( Y=\tfrac{6}{s(s+3)} \); splitting by partial fractions into \( \tfrac2s-\tfrac{2}{s+3} \) inverts to \( y=2-2e^{-3t} \). The constant \(2\) is the steady state, the \(-2e^{-3t}\) the transient — the whole solution, initial condition included, produced by algebra.

§4 · The s-domain and the method

The ratio of output transform to input transform, with zero initial conditions, is the transfer function — the compact description of a system in the \( s \)-domain that control engineering is built on. The location of its poles (the roots of the denominator) is the system's stability: poles with negative real part mean decay, imaginary poles mean oscillation, exactly the eigenvalue reading of Lesson 7 in another language. The working method never changes: (1) transform every term, folding in initial data; (2) solve the algebraic equation for \( Y(s) \); (3) break \( Y(s) \) into table pieces by partial fractions and invert. Master this on the smooth cases here, and the same recipe handles the switched and impulsive inputs that real systems face.

Forced first-order

Problem. Solve \( y'+3y=6 \), \( y(0)=0 \), and identify the steady state.
quiz.solution: Solution. Transform: \( sY+3Y=\dfrac6s\Rightarrow Y=\dfrac{6}{s(s+3)} \). Partial fractions: \( \dfrac{2}{s}-\dfrac{2}{s+3} \). Invert: \( y=2-2e^{-3t} \). Steady state \(=2\); the transient \(-2e^{-3t}\) carries \(y\) from \(0\) up to \(2\).

quiz.q: \( \mathcal{L}\{e^{3t}\} \) equals:
The derivative rule \( \mathcal{L}\{y'\} \) equals:
quiz.solution: \( \mathcal{L}\{y'\}=sY(s)-y(0) \): each derivative becomes a factor of \(s\), initial value subtracted.

quiz.q: \( \mathcal{L}\{t\} \) equals:
The three Laplace steps for an IVP are:
quiz.solution: Transform the ODE (with initial data), solve the algebraic equation for \( Y(s) \), invert with the table.

quiz.q: \( \mathcal{L}\{\sin 4t\} \) equals:
quiz.solution: \( \mathcal{L}\{\sin\omega t\}=\omega/(s^2+\omega^2) \) with \( \omega=4 \).

quiz.q: Transforming \( y'+2y=0,\ y(0)=3 \) gives \( Y(s)= \):
quiz.solution: \( (s+2)Y=3\Rightarrow Y=3/(s+2)\Rightarrow y=3e^{-2t} \).

quiz.q: The inverse transform of \( \frac{1}{s^2+9} \) is:
quiz.solution: Since \( \mathcal{L}\{\sin3t\}=3/(s^2+9) \), dividing by 3 gives \( \tfrac13\sin3t \).

quiz.q: The Laplace transform turns differentiation into:
quiz.solution: \( \mathcal{L}\{y'\}=sY-y(0) \): a derivative becomes a factor of \(s\) — the whole point of the method.

10.kuwait:
A controls or instrumentation engineer lives in the s-domain. When tuning a temperature loop or a level controller, the process and the controller are each written as transfer functions — output-over-input in \(s\) — and the loop's behaviour is read from where the combined poles sit. Poles in the left half-plane (negative real part) mean the loop settles; a pole crossing to the right means it will hunt or run away. The Laplace method is what turns a plant's differential equations into those algebraic transfer functions in the first place, which is why every controller datasheet and tuning rule is stated in \(s\).

The step-input case is the everyday test. Bumping a setpoint and watching the response is applying a step \( \tfrac{1}{s} \); the observed rise and settling are the inverse transform of the transfer function times that step. Reading whether the response is sluggish, snappy, or oscillatory — and adjusting the controller to fix it — is exactly the transform-solve-invert cycle of this lesson, run on real hardware.

11.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Integration of sines and cosines — the coefficient integrals.
  • Period and frequency — \( \omega_0=2\pi/T \).
  • Sinusoids — the harmonic building blocks.

Toolkit — laws and formulas

QuantityFormulaReads as
Fourier series\( \tfrac{a_0}{2}+\sum(a_n\cos n\omega_0 t+b_n\sin n\omega_0 t) \)Signal = sum of harmonics.
Fundamental\( \omega_0=2\pi/T \)Lowest frequency present.

quiz.q:
Square-wave harmonics

Problem. For an odd square wave of amplitude \(1\), find the amplitudes \( b_1,b_3,b_5 \) and the ratio \( b_3/b_1 \).
quiz.solution: Solution. \( b_n=\dfrac{4}{n\pi} \) for odd \(n\): \( b_1=\dfrac{4}{\pi}\approx1.27 \), \( b_3=\dfrac{4}{3\pi}\approx0.42 \), \( b_5=\dfrac{4}{5\pi}\approx0.25 \). The ratio \( b_3/b_1=1/3 \) — higher harmonics fall off as \(1/n\).

quiz.q:
Fundamental frequency

Problem. A machine vibration repeats every \( T=0.02 \) s. Find its fundamental frequency in rad/s and in Hz.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( \omega_0=\dfrac{2\pi}{T}=\dfrac{2\pi}{0.02}=100\pi\approx314 \) rad/s. In hertz, \( f_0=\dfrac1T=\dfrac{1}{0.02}=50 \) Hz — so the spectrum's lowest line sits at \(50\) Hz (e.g. a machine turning at \(3000\) rpm).

quiz.q:
Which harmonics survive?

Problem. State which harmonics an odd square wave contains and why, and give the amplitude of the 3rd relative to the 1st.
quiz.solution: Solution. Only odd harmonics (1, 3, 5, …). Its half-wave symmetry makes all even coefficients integrate to zero, and being odd it has sine terms only. The 3rd harmonic has amplitude \( b_3/b_1=1/3 \) of the fundamental.

quiz.q: A Fourier series expresses a periodic signal as a sum of:
quiz.solution: It is a sum of sinusoids at the fundamental \( \omega_0 \) and its integer multiples.

quiz.q: The fundamental frequency of a signal of period \(T\) is:
quiz.solution: \( \omega_0=2\pi/T \); in hertz \( f_0=1/T \).

quiz.q: Fourier coefficients are found by:
choices: integrating the signal against each harmonic over one period
quiz.solution: Orthogonality makes \( b_n=\tfrac2T\int_0^T f\sin n\omega_0 t\,dt \) isolate exactly the \(n\)th amount.

quiz.q: An odd square wave contains:
quiz.solution: Its half-wave symmetry kills the even harmonics; only 1, 3, 5, … remain.

quiz.q: The harmonic amplitudes of a square wave fall off as:
quiz.solution: \( b_n=4/(n\pi) \), so amplitude \(\propto 1/n\).

quiz.q: A vibration FFT shows discrete lines because the signal is:
quiz.solution: A periodic signal has energy only at the fundamental and its harmonics — those are the lines.

quiz.q: Adding more harmonics to a square-wave approximation:
choices: sharpens the corners (with slight Gibbs overshoot)
quiz.solution: More terms sharpen the jumps toward the true square, leaving a small persistent overshoot at the discontinuities (Gibbs).

quiz.q: In machine diagnostics, a new spectral line at a fault frequency signals:
quiz.solution: Extra energy at a bearing or gear-mesh harmonic is the Fourier signature of a growing fault.

11.kuwait:
A condition-monitoring technician stares at a Fourier spectrum for a living. The running speed of a pump or motor sets the fundamental line, and every fault has a harmonic fingerprint: imbalance shows at \(1\times\) the speed, misalignment at \(2\times\), a bearing defect at its own characteristic frequency, gear wear at the mesh frequency and its sidebands. Because the vibration is periodic, all of this energy lands on discrete lines — exactly the Fourier coefficients of this lesson — so reading the heights and positions of those lines is reading the health of the machine.

The same decomposition underlies power quality on a plant's electrical supply. A distorted current waveform is a fundamental at \(50\) Hz plus harmonics, and the odd harmonics — the very ones a square-like waveform carries — are what overheat transformers and trip protection. Measuring the harmonic amplitudes and keeping the distortion within limits is a Fourier calculation turned into a maintenance and compliance task.
